description: NCERT Class 7 Ch 13 + Class 8 Ch 12 (Exponents and Powers — 8 laws), Class 9 Ch 1 (rational exponents and surds), and JEE Mains/Advanced + IIT-level olympiad problems.  Covers all 8 laws of exponents, scientific notation, exponential equations, compound interest, exponential growth/decay, and algebraic exponent identities.

questions.expression_latex: \text{Let } x = a^{1/(b-c)}, y = a^{1/(c-a)}, z = a^{1/(a-b)}. \text{ Prove } xyz = 1.
questions.question_text: (IIT-JEE) Add the exponents: 1/(b−c) + 1/(c−a) + 1/(a−b).
questions.answer_latex: \text{The three exponents sum to 0 (common denominator: the polynomial in a, b, c factors symmetrically).  Hence } xyz = a^0 = 1. \blacksquare

questions.expression_latex: \text{If } a^x = b^y = c^z, \text{ and } a, b, c \text{ are in geometric progression, prove that } x, y, z \text{ are in harmonic progression.}
questions.question_text: (IIT-JEE) Use the GP relation b² = ac plus the log-of-equality manipulation.
questions.answer_latex: \text{Let } a^x = b^y = c^z = K; \; \log a = \log K / x, \log b = \log K / y, \log c = \log K / z. \text{GP: } 2 \log b = \log a + \log c \Rightarrow \frac{2}{y} = \frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{z}; \; \text{i.e. } x, y, z \text{ in HP.} \blacksquare
questions.answer_plain: Use 2/y = 1/x + 1/z which is the HP condition.

(Putnam classic) Let L be the limit.  Then L = (√2)^L; solve for L by inspection or via x = a^x.
questions.answer_latex: L = (\sqrt 2)^L \Rightarrow \log L = L \log \sqrt 2 = (L/2) \log 2; \;\; \text{the equation } x = a^x \text{ has solutions } x = 2 \text{ when } a = \sqrt 2; \;\; \text{converges to } L = 2.
